text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION — THIRD-LINE THERAPY RESPONSE ASSESSMENT

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Marcus T. Delgado is a 59-year-old male with known metastatic adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon (KRAS G12D-mutant, microsatellite stable) with hepatic and pulmonary metastases. Patient has received prior FOLFOX/Avastin (12 cycles, discontinued due to cumulative oxali neuropathy), maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin/bev, and FOLFIRI/Cyramza (progressed after approximately 7 months). Currently on third-line TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil) plus bev — this examination is obtained after completion of 3 cycles for restaging purposes. Comparison is made to prior CT dated at the time of third-line therapy initiation (approximately 3 months prior), as well as earlier examinations for longitudinal reference.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast at 3 mL/sec. Images were acquired in the portal venous phase through the abdomen and pelvis, and in the standard chest phase through the thorax. Axial images were reconstructed at 2.5 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats. Oral contrast was not administered. Scout topogram reviewed. Dosimetry: CTDIvol 14.2 mGy; DLP 512 mGy·cm.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules are again identified, consistent with the previously documented metastatic pulmonary disease. The dominant nodule in the right lower lobe now measures approximately 1.2 cm in greatest axial dimension, representing a minimal increase compared to 1.1 cm on the prior study. This nodule demonstrates a rounded, well-circumscribed morphology with soft tissue attenuation and no internal calcification or cavitation. The right upper lobe nodule remains stable at 0.7 cm. Three additional smaller nodules documented at the prior examination — in the left lower lobe (0.8 cm), right middle lobe (0.6 cm), and left upper lobe lingular segment (0.5 cm) — are redemonstrated and appear grossly unchanged. No new pulmonary nodules are identified beyond those previously documented. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. No endobronchial lesions. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The mediastinal and hilar contours are unremarkable. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymph nodes by size criteria. Cardiac silhouette is within normal limits. The visualized great vessels are patent. No pericardial effusion.

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures:
No destructive osseous lesions identified in the imaged thoracic skeleton. Mild degenerative changes at the thoracic spine, age-appropriate. No rib lesions.

---

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver:
The liver is mildly enlarged, measuring approximately 19.2 cm in craniocaudal span on the coronal reconstruction. Multiple hepatic metastases are again identified. Interval changes are described as follows relative to the prior restaging CT at the time of disease progression (initiation of third-line therapy):

— Segment VI lesion: Previously measured 2.9 cm at the time of progression on FOLFIRI/Cyramza. On the current examination, this lesion measures approximately 3.1 cm in greatest axial dimension, representing a slight but discernible increase in size. The lesion demonstrates heterogeneous enhancement with a relatively hypodense center and peripheral rim enhancement, consistent with central necrosis within a hypervascular metastatic deposit. No change in morphology to suggest hemorrhage.

— Segment V lesion: This lesion, first identified at the time of second-line therapy progression and measuring 0.8 cm, now measures approximately 0.9 cm. Minimal interval enlargement.

— Segment IVa lesion: Redemonstrated, measuring 1.1 cm, essentially unchanged compared to prior examination.

— Segment II lesion: Stable at 0.7 cm. Low-attenuation focus on portal venous phase images with subtle peripheral enhancement.

— Segment VII lesion: The smallest target lesion, previously 0.4 cm, again identified at approximately 0.4 cm. Unchanged.

No new hepatic lesions are definitively identified on this examination beyond those already catalogued. The portal and hepatic veins are patent without evidence of tumor thrombus. No biliary ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is present without cholelithiasis or wall thickening. The common bile duct is normal in caliber.

Spleen:
Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is normal in size, contour, and enhancement pattern. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic lymphadenopathy.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size, shape, and enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. Simple-appearing cortical cysts noted bilaterally, unchanged from prior examinations.

Retroperitoneum and Lymph Nodes:
No new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. A small paraaortic lymph node cluster in the mid-retroperitoneum previously noted measures up to 0.8 cm in short axis, stable. No discrete mesenteric adenopathy above size criteria.

Bowel:
The sigmoid colon primary tumor is again visualized as an area of focal circumferential wall thickening in the sigmoid colon measuring approximately 3.2 cm in greatest dimension on axial imaging, mildly increased from 3.1 cm at the prior study, though this measurement has remained relatively stable over the preceding several months. Pericolonic fat stranding persists in this region; the stranding does not appear appreciably different from prior. The remainder of the visualized large and small bowel demonstrates no obstruction, no pneumatosis, and no free air. The appendix is not definitively visualized; no appendiceal pathology is suspected.

Peritoneum and Ascites:
No ascites identified on this examination. No peritoneal studding or omental cake. The peritoneal surfaces are smooth. This is notable given the patient's increasing tumor burden, and while reassuring at this time point, careful surveillance at subsequent examinations is warranted.

Pelvis:
The urinary bladder demonstrates normal wall thickness and fills appropriately with contrast. The prostate appears mildly enlarged, consistent with the patient's age and known demographics; no suspicious prostatic lesions are identified on this limited assessment. No pelvic lymphadenopathy.

Bones and Soft Tissues:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions are identified throughout the imaged skeleton. Mild multilevel lumbar spondylosis with small osteophytes, expected for age. No pathological fractures. No soft tissue masses. Subcutaneous fat is diminished compared to the baseline examination from approximately 20 months prior, consistent with the clinical history of weight loss and declining nutritional status.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma — multiple sites of involvement as described. Overall, the degree of change on this examination is minimal, with the disease appearing largely similar to the prior study from approximately 3 months ago. Slight size increases in the dominant hepatic segment VI metastasis (3.1 cm, up from 2.9 cm) and the segment V hepatic lesion (0.9 cm, up from 0.8 cm) are noted, though these changes are at the threshold of measurement reproducibility. Remaining hepatic lesions are essentially stable.

2. Bilateral pulmonary metastases — at least five nodules identified in total, the largest now 1.2 cm in the right lower lobe representing minimal interval increase from 1.1 cm. No new pulmonary nodules detected. The pulmonary disease component appears overall stable.

3. Sigmoid colon primary mass — mildly enlarged at approximately 3.2 cm compared to 3.1 cm on prior study; this change is within the margin of measurement variability and is considered clinically stable. No evidence of obstruction.

4. No new sites of metastatic disease identified on this examination. No ascites. No peritoneal carcinomatosis.

5. Taken in sum, the overall appearance represents a disease state that is marginally changed, with what appears to be very slow incremental growth across several measured lesions rather than any dramatic interval change. The trajectory of disease over the longer-term course remains one of gradual progression despite multiple lines of therapy, and the cumulative findings across multiple examinations should be weighed carefully in the clinical context of the patient's overall condition and treatment goals.

Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Division]
Technologist: [Radiology Technologist]
Report finalized electronically.
